films.title: Master
films.synopsis: In this timely, incisive Rotterdam award winner, an idealistic rural schoolteacher turns local politician – then slowly submits to the inexorable weight of corruption.
films.description: In a remote Bangladeshi community surrounded by protected woodlands, beloved schoolteacher Jahir (Nasir Uddin Khan) is running in the local election. With his progressive values and gift for eloquent oration, Jahir seems like the perfect progressive candidate, a voice who can represent this rural community on the national level. Soon, however, his ideals come into conflict with crushing reality, as campaign donors, influential businessmen, local thugs, shady police, petty bureaucrats and political powerbrokers all lean on Jahir for favours, preferential treatment and dirty deals. The recipient of the Big Screen Award at Rotterdam, this sober political drama is a morality play of compromised ethics and the slow rot of corruption. The son of a former government minister, director Rezwan Shahriar Sumit knows all too well the personal cost of a political career. Openly evoking the real-life controversies of former prime minister Sheikh Hasina’s authoritarian rule, Master is a classical political parable that’s both local in its specifics and universal in its themes.
films.awards: In this timely, incisive Rotterdam award winner, an idealistic rural schoolteacher turns local politician – then slowly submits to the inexorable weight of corruption. | The recipient of the Big Screen Award at Rotterdam, this sober political drama is a morality play of compromised ethics and the slow rot of corruption.

films.title: Waking Hours
films.synopsis: A nocturnal descent into the lives of Afghan people smugglers living in the forests of the Balkan route, this Venice award winner is a profound, immersive experience.
films.description: In the darkness of a forest in northern Serbia, right by the border of Hungary, Afghan passeurs gather around a flickering fire: smoking, baking bread and discussing the best corridors for smuggling people across the border. As gunshots resound in the distance and loudspeakers blast periodic warnings from the Hungarian side, these tiny figures linger in the shadow of the European Union. Taking place almost entirely in darkness – illuminated only by firelight, torches, phones and distant passing headlights – this immersive, experiential work of direct cinema takes us into the world of the men who facilitate safe passage along the Balkan route. Seeing the work of their often-reviled subjects as “a response to and a consequence of Europe’s exclusionary policies”, Italian directors Federico Cammarata and Filippo Foscarini covertly film from a distance, danger forever in the air. With its nocturnal imagery and heightened quality of sound, Waking Hours plays almost like a slow-burning horror movie: plunging viewers into an unsettling night; cultivating a sense of mounting fear; asking us to imagine what’s lurking outside the frame.
films.awards: A nocturnal descent into the lives of Afghan people smugglers living in the forests of the Balkan route, this Venice award winner is a profound, immersive experience.
films.recommendation: Letterboxd 3.26/5；纪录片、实验 / 纪录新视野 / 澳洲首映。推荐理由：政治与社会现实、恐怖 / 超自然。奖项/展映线索：获奖、威尼斯

films.title: Everybody Digs Bill Evans
films.synopsis: In a virtuosic riff on a biopic standard, this Silver Bear–winning ensemble piece plays on the emotions of the people surrounding an anguished US jazz piano icon.
films.description: June 1961: the Bill Evans Trio have just live-recorded a New York residency that will spawn two celebrated jazz albums. Ten days later, a car crash kills bassist Scott LaFaro at just 25. Devastated, Evans can’t even touch a piano. He stays with his brother Harry, sister-in-law Pat and adoring niece Debby; shoots heroin with his girlfriend Ellaine; and journeys silently, scarily inward. Bill’s depressive black hole starts to claim Harry – a music conservatory teacher who envies his kid brother’s genius – and even shadows their working-class parents’ sunny Florida retirement. British documentarian Grant Gee ( Joy Division ) won the Berlinale’s Silver Bear for Best Director with this fiction debut, whose jaunty title – borrowed from an early Evans album – belies its deeper themes. In elegant black-and-white, the film seeks its lost subject in negative space, emulating his subtle style; impressionistic montages shimmer like harmonic chords and slide across chronologies like modal progressions. Playing both Evans and his compositions, frequent Joachim Trier collaborator Anders Danielsen Lie ( The Worst Person in the World , MIFF 2021) is magnetically quiet, while Bill Pullman and Laurie Metcalf are in career-best form as Bill’s parents.
films.awards: In a virtuosic riff on a biopic standard, this Silver Bear–winning ensemble piece plays on the emotions of the people surrounding an anguished US jazz piano icon. | British documentarian Grant Gee ( Joy Division ) won the Berlinale’s Silver Bear for Best Director with this fiction debut, whose jaunty title – borrowed from an early Evans album – belies its deeper themes.
films.recommendation: Letterboxd 3.23/5；剧情、音乐 / 音乐电影 / 澳洲首映。推荐理由：家庭关系、政治与社会现实、音乐与表演。奖项/展映线索：银熊奖、导演奖、获奖、柏林电影节

films.title: Wishful Thinking
films.synopsis: This SXSW Grand Jury Award–winning high-concept rom-com playfully questions the notion that love is the most powerful force in existence … for better or worse.
films.description: Charlie and Julia are deeply in love. They are also fundamentally incompatible – Charlie wants to get married and start a family; Julia doesn’t – and they spend as much time fighting as they do making up. Desperate to fix their relationship, they visit twin TikTok love gurus Tilly and Tilly, whose mysterious counsel reminds the couple how and why they fell for each other. It also unlocks something else, with earth-shattering repercussions … literally. Cheekily interpreting the real-world implications of Rhonda Byrne’s pseudoscientific self-help hit The Secret , first-time feature director Graham Parkes creates a wonderfully witty, lived-in love story with a supernatural through-line that’s as hilarious as it is horrifying. Utilising ingenious split-screen techniques and capturing the stunning vistas of the Pacific Northwest, Parkes and cinematographer Christopher Ripley ensure the film looks incredible, while Lewis Pullman ( The Testament of Ann Lee ) and Maya Hawke ( Once Upon a Time in Hollywood , MIFF 2019; Stranger Things ) are phenomenal as Charlie and Julia, exuding chemistry while anchoring every emotional heartbeat and delivering every punchline to perfection. With a supporting cast that includes Randall Park, Kate Berlant and Jon Hamm as himself in a brilliantly executed recurring bit, Wishful Thinking is a boldly original, funny and insightful study of the wonderful delusion at the heart of all love: that its exhilarating highs and heartbreaking lows can change the world.
films.awards: This SXSW Grand Jury Award–winning high-concept rom-com playfully questions the notion that love is the most powerful force in existence … for better or worse.
films.recommendation: Letterboxd 3.72/5；剧情、喜剧、爱情、奇幻 / 主打佳片 / 澳洲首映。推荐理由：家庭关系、亲密关系、政治与社会现实、恐怖 / 超自然。奖项/展映线索：获奖

films.title: Jim Queen
films.synopsis: Come for a gleefully filthy romp through Gay Paree as a buff influencer and his twink fight to cure a heinous virus that makes queer people straight!
films.description: Jim Parfait is the top gay gymfluencer in Paris. When he’s not posting his daily workouts, he’s flaunting his gigantic rig at parties with his best pal Nina. She’s the first to spot that one of Jim’s 24 magnificent abs has vanished. Quelle horreur! Jim has heterosis: the dreaded virus that’s turning queer Parisians … straight . As Jim’s fickle followers abandon him for his brawny nemesis Pavel, he teams up with Lucien – closeted twink son of France’s villainous conservative health minister – on a hero’s cruise for a cure. In the city’s degenerate demimonde, they’ll encounter bears, techno-blasting leather puppies, Glamydia of the Sisters of Perpetual Indulgence and the militant ‘Gaystapo’ threatening Jim with reverse-conversion therapy. There’s serious commentary on the state of the modern world to be found in Nicolas Athane and Marco Nguyen’s outrageous Cannes-premiering satire. But be prepared nonetheless for a barrage of the world’s silliest, lewdest jokes, while the macaron-toned Rick and Morty -esque animation stuffs the frame with as much absurdity as the protein that Jim gorges on.
films.awards: There’s serious commentary on the state of the modern world to be found in Nicolas Athane and Marco Nguyen’s outrageous Cannes-premiering satire.
films.recommendation: Letterboxd 4.01/5；动画、喜剧 / 动画单元 / 维州首映。推荐理由：家庭关系。奖项/展映线索：戛纳、首映

films.title: The Cycle of Love
films.synopsis: To find the Swedish girl who’d won his heart, a young Indian artist set out to cross the world – by bike. Find out what happened next in this moving true story.
films.description: When P.K. Mahanandia was a baby, an astrologer predicted he’d marry a woman from outside India. But how? Mahanandia was Dalit – the lowest, poorest caste. Then, in 1975, Swedish backpacker Lotta von Schedvin paid the gentle, curious 23-year-old to sketch her portrait on a Delhi street, and soon fell for him. After two magical months together, von Schedvin returned home – but for Mahanandia, it wasn’t over. With his last $80, he bought a secondhand Raleigh bicycle and pedalled off on the hippie trail on what would become a 6000-mile quest. The Proclaimers would’ve given up long before … but, for this Tour de Romance, artistic talent and the kindness of many strangers were more than enough fuel to keep the wheels turning. Documentarian Orlando von Einsiedel ( Evelyn , MIFF 2019) is always looking for the best aspects of humanity, whether it be among gorilla-protecting Congolese rangers in Virunga (MIFF 2014) or Syria’s volunteer civil defenders in his 2016 Oscar-winning short The White Helmets . Here, he weaves the couple’s photos, letters and heartfelt interviews through beguilingly vintage-textured re-enactments of their courtship and Mahanandia’s odyssey, adding unscripted interactions with some of the colourful characters the director meets while filming along his subject’s original route. Be prepared to fall head over handlebars for these dreamers.
films.awards: To find the Swedish girl who’d won his heart, a young Indian artist set out to cross the world – by bike. | ...Congolese rangers in Virunga (MIFF 2014) or Syria’s volunteer civil defenders in his 2016 Oscar-winning short The White Helmets ....

films.title: The Tale of Silyan
films.synopsis: Man meets stork in this heartwarming depiction of rural struggle and rehabilitation from one of the filmmakers behind the Oscar-nominated Honeyland.
films.description: Nikola Conev, born into a long lineage of farmers in North Macedonia, is struggling. Harsh economic conditions, the collapse of local markets and the decline of rural living have gripped his local community. His wife, children and grandchildren have all left the country for greater opportunities. At a loss, and filled with loneliness, he brings home a white stork with a broken wing. As he nurses it back to health, Conev experiences his own sense of healing. Tamara Kotevska’s previous portrait of farming in rural North Macedonia, Honeyland (co-directed with Ljubomir Stefanov, MIFF 2019), received Oscar nominations for both Best Documentary and Best International Feature Film. The Tale of Silyan , which premiered at Venice and won Best Feature Documentary at the International Documentary Association Awards, is equally impressive: a unique modern-day folktale filled with heart, humour and spectacular cinematography.
films.awards: Man meets stork in this heartwarming depiction of rural struggle and rehabilitation from one of the filmmakers behind the Oscar-nominated Honeyland. | ...ural North Macedonia, Honeyland (co-directed with Ljubomir Stefanov, MIFF 2019), received Oscar nominations for both Best Documentary and Best International Feature Film....

films.title: Remake
films.synopsis: In his most devastatingly personal work to date – awarded at Venice and True/False – veteran auto-documentarian Ross McElwee probes his home movies for insights into his son’s death.
films.description: Adrian McElwee was just 27 when he died, in 2016 – a victim of the opioid crisis, having also struggled with bipolar disorder. He’d figured prominently in his father’s previous film, 2011’s Photographic Memory , in which the filmmaker was prompted by his growing distance from Adrian to retrace the steps of a sojourn from his own youth. In the painful aftermath of Adrian’s death, McElwee turned to his archive, in search of what he and his camera might have missed. Offsetting this emotion-laden investigation is another, almost satirical plot line involving a proposed adaptation of the film that put the director on the map, Sherman’s March (MIFF 1986). Together, the two threads point towards an inability to exert control over one’s own progeny. A clear influence on the likes of John Wilson ( The History of Concrete , MIFF 2026), McElwee, now in his late seventies, has long been an obsessive documenter of his own life, always bringing a wry intelligence and, at times, endearingly awkward humour to his filmed pursuits. In the more sober and reflective Remake , however, he finds himself questioning the limits of his chosen medium.
films.awards: In his most devastatingly personal work to date – awarded at Venice and True/False – veteran auto-documentarian Ross McElwee probes his home movies for insights into his son’s death.
films.recommendation: Letterboxd 3.99/5；纪录片 / 纪录片单元 / 维州首映。推荐理由：家庭关系、成长与青春、政治与社会现实、记忆与历史、失去与哀悼。奖项/展映线索：获奖、威尼斯

films.title: My Wife Cries
films.synopsis: The latest from leading Berlin School writer/director Angela Schanelec follows a middle-aged couple whose marriage unravels after a car accident brings a secret to light.
films.description: At work one day, crane operator Thomas receives a call from his wife, Carla. She’s been in a car accident and needs him to pick her up. Arriving at the hospital, he finds her on a park bench outside, physically unharmed but crying inconsolably. Eventually, Carla confesses something about the specific circumstances of the accident, leaving Thomas shattered. This revelation leads both Thomas and Carla to share intimate conversations with friends and loved ones about the fragility of romantic relationships and the impossibility of ever truly knowing someone – even those with whom we share a life. Two-time Silver Bear–winning filmmaker Angela Schanelec ( I Was at Home, But , MIFF 2019) returns with another characteristically austere, defiantly minimalist work that’s fascinated with the psychology of modern relationships but resists easy interpretation – the kind of filmmaking that will appeal to fans of directors like Hong Sang-soo, Aki Kaurismäki and Éric Rohmer. Shot on 35mm by regular Radu Jude collaborator Marius Panduru ( Diary of a Chambermaid , MIFF 2026), the Berlinale Competition–selected film is constructed from formally controlled tableau-like sequences, each deeply attuned to the beauty of everyday life, whether it be a brass band playing in a park as it begins to rain or a group of friends performing a dance to a Leonard Cohen song.

films.title: All of a Sudden
films.synopsis: Virginie Efira and Tao Okamoto shared Best Actress Awards at Cannes for their sterling work in the latest long-form opus from master filmmaker Ryusuke Hamaguchi.
films.description: Marie-Lou (Virginie Efira, Parallel Tales , MIFF 2026) runs an aged-care facility on the outskirts of Paris. She’s devoted to her job and her patients, and firmly believes that the elderly should be treated with dignity. It’s tough work, and, like so many of her employees, she’s dealing with burnout. But her spirit is rekindled when she meets Japanese playwright Mari (Tao Okamoto), who’s staging a theatre piece about the history of mental asylums in Italy. As they plunge headlong into all-night conversations about mortality, philosophy and the degradations of capitalism, Mari and Marie-Lou’s new friendship will change both of their lives. The films of Ryusuke Hamaguchi – often in-depth explorations of human connection fostered by theatre and workshops – operate at a distinctive register and rhythm, and his first film made outside of Japan is no exception. In All of a Sudden , Hamaguchi interrogates the state of elder care, using it to reflect the ways contemporary societies function. His mastery meets its match in leads Efira and Okamoto, who shared the Best Actress award at Cannes for remarkable performances that shift between French and Japanese and work through a complex array of emotions.
films.awards: Virginie Efira and Tao Okamoto shared Best Actress Awards at Cannes for their sterling work in the latest long-form opus from master filmmaker Ryusuke Hamaguchi. | His mastery meets its match in leads Efira and Okamoto, who shared the Best Actress award at Cannes for remarkable performances that shift between French and Japanese and work through a complex array of emotions.

films.title: The Match
films.synopsis: The mythology of the fateful 1986 World Cup quarter-final clash between England and Argentina is untangled in this thrilling documentary.
films.description: The date 22 June 1986 is etched into the collective mythologies of two nations and football fans around the globe. The FIFA World Cup quarter-final match played that day between arch rivals England and Argentina is infamous for the contentious “hand of god” goal from Diego Maradona, followed minutes later by his “goal of the century”. But it’s not just the golden boy’s feats that set the match apart; geopolitical tensions that had been brewing for years had only recently boiled over in the deadly Falklands War, and fans on both sides viewed this game through a prism of revenge. Forty years later, directors Juan Cabral and Santiago Franco reinvestigate this historic moment in exhilarating detail, echoing the structure of the game and thoughtfully reflecting on the historical, cultural and sociopolitical context that gave it so much charge. Cathartic interviews with former on-field opponents including Gary Lineker, Peter Shilton, Ricardo Giusti, Jorge Burruchaga and Julio Olarticoechea, shot in exquisite monochrome, are juxtaposed against lively archival footage of the match in vivid colour, all framed in the same boxy 4:3 ratio through which that day’s audiences viewed the action on their TV sets. Whether you’ve seen the match 100 times or never, The Match makes for a suspenseful, riveting and ultimately moving watch.

films.title: Bitter Christmas
films.synopsis: Pedro Almodóvar returns with this witty, award-winning metafiction that satirises the vampiric nature of creativity.
films.description: It’s 2004, and the wells of inspiration are drying up for movie director Elsa. Worse still, despite her firefighter/stripper boyfriend’s coddling, she’s as burdened by migraines as she is with her friends’ troubles. But all of this is merely a story; a creation of filmmaker Raúl, Elsa is the protagonist of the film that he’s struggling to complete in 2026, much to the dismay of his supportive partner, Santi. That spark suddenly returns when Raúl’s assistant Mónica takes leave to help a friend who has just lost a child, a tragedy that becomes a plot point in his own script. Perhaps this new inspiration will give Raúl insights into his own life and relationships; but how far is too far when an artist continually cribs from the experiences of others? Much like Pain and Glory (MIFF 2019), Pedro Almodóvar’s latest Competition headliner at Cannes – where it shared the Soundtrack Award – is an ingenious piece of self-portraiture that interrogates creativity and its impediments. The enthralling film-within-a-film explodes onto the screen with the director’s signature vivid colours and sumptuous settings, which are enhanced by the piquant soundtrack by long-time collaborator Alberto Iglesias. Leads Bárbara Lennie and Leonardo Sbaraglia are stellar as mirror images of each other and of Almodóvar himself, propelling an autofictional triumph that ponders art’s (mis)appropriation of life.
films.awards: Pedro Almodóvar returns with this witty, award-winning metafiction that satirises the vampiric nature of creativity. | Much like Pain and Glory (MIFF 2019), Pedro Almodóvar’s latest Competition headliner at Cannes – where it shared the Soundtrack Award – is an ingenious piece of self-portraiture that interrogates creativity and its impedimen...

films.title: Who Are You, Polly Maggoo?
films.synopsis: Legendary fashion photographer and photojournalist William Klein exploded into narrative filmmaking with this Prix Jean Vigo–winning mockumentary, now a cult record of groovy 1960s mod fashion.
films.description: Trailed by a gormless French film crew through Paris, American model Polly (played by director William Klein’s favourite model, Dorothy McGowan) is a mod Alice weaving her way through a stylishly shambolic Wonderland. Right from the famous opening sequence – a runway show in a Gaudi-esque colosseum in which fashionistas coo over models encased in sheet metal – Klein is fascinated with fashion’s theatre of the absurd. His satirical eye finds something solemn, even religious, in the banal pronouncements of fashion editors and the nonsensical questions of TV interviewers, while Polly seems bemused to find herself an object of worship for trend-hungry teens and lecherous men. Two years before he would make the unnerving American satire Mr. Freedom , Klein skewers the rarefied world of Parisian haute couture and its hangers-on from his privileged position as one of the most subversive photographers to appear in American Vogue . With his sights on the commercialisation of the ‘60s “youthquake”, he mashes up the French New Wave with Beatles caper A Hard Day’s Night , using playful animation that anticipates Monty Python’s Flying Circus .
films.awards: Legendary fashion photographer and photojournalist William Klein exploded into narrative filmmaking with this Prix Jean Vigo–winning mockumentary, now a cult record of groovy 1960s mod fashion.
films.recommendation: Letterboxd 3.74/5；Mockumentary / Ulrike Ottinger: Radical Travels。推荐理由：成长与青春、失去与哀悼。奖项/展映线索：获奖

films.title: Eight Bridges
films.synopsis: James Benning, America’s master of slow cinema, invites audiences to consider bridges: structural and political, physical and metaphorical
films.description: James Benning, America’s master of slow cinema, invites audiences to consider bridges: structural and political, physical and metaphorical. Eight American bridges – San Francisco’s Golden Gate; New Mexico’s Rio Grande Gorge; Selma, Alabama’s Edmund Pettus; Florida’s Seven Mile; the George Washington that connects New Jersey and Manhattan; the Dubuque-Wisconsin that crosses the Mississippi River; North Dakota’s Hi-Line; and the Astoria-Megler running from Oregon to Washington – are viewed for 10 minutes each. Each shot is simultaneously static and full of life, the long takes painting with light, sound and composition the point where nature meets human ingenuity, activity and history.
films.awards: Eight American bridges – San Francisco’s Golden Gate; New Mexico’s Rio Grande Gorge; Selma, Alabama’s Edmund Pettus; Florida’s Seven Mile; the George Washington that connects Ne...

films.title: Mary Oliver: Saved by the Beauty of the World
films.synopsis: Bestselling Pulitzer Prize–winning poet Mary Oliver was famously private; a loving look at her one wild and precious life sheds new light on her cherished work.
films.description: If a winner of major contemporary poetry awards were ever simultaneously a pop-cultural icon, Mary Oliver is it. Openly queer but fiercely private, Oliver – who died in 2019, aged 83 – was an American mystic, enamoured by the transcendental natural beauty she encountered on her daily walks through Provincetown’s woods and wilds. Influenced by Whitman, Thoreau, Keats and Emerson, and often compared with Dickinson and Rumi, Oliver’s celebrated nature poems have inspired ordinary folk for generations. Now, in this documentary on the poet’s life, director Sasha Waters respectfully shows us the woman behind the words adored by millions, exploring Oliver’s lonely and sometimes traumatic childhood as well as her salvation: in love, in nature and in writing. Friends and admirers including Stephen Colbert, Lucy Dacus, John Waters, Steve Buscemi and Helena Bonham Carter share memories and read favourite poems, while previously unseen photos, prose, essays and correspondence offer insights into Oliver’s inner world. Interspersed with stunning visuals of the landscapes that so inspired her, the film offers a tender, authentic and enlivening study of her life, loves and letters.
films.awards: Bestselling Pulitzer Prize–winning poet Mary Oliver was famously private; a loving look at her one wild and precious life sheds new light on her cherished work. | If a winner of major contemporary poetry awards were ever simultaneously a pop-cultural icon, Mary Oliver is it.

films.title: The History of Concrete
films.synopsis: When an inventive cult filmmaker decides to make a Hallmark-style documentary on concrete, he paves a path to something far more profound and hilarious.
films.description: Idle during the 2023 Writers Guild of America strike, documentarian John Wilson attends a workshop on how to make Hallmark Christmas rom-com movies. Inspired by his apartment building’s crumbling foundations, he tests this aspirational, escapist formula on the world’s love affair with concrete. Unsurprisingly, Wilson struggles to pitch his idea to prospective investors – and, fretting that he might be falling through the industry’s cracks, he takes a pilgrimage from the celebrity handprints outside LA’s Chinese Theatre to the dome atop Rome’s ancient Pantheon and more, finding surprising insights along the way. Fans of HBO comedy series How To With John Wilson will rejoice that the director’s Sundance-premiering debut feature continues along his curious perambulation. But whether you want eccentric tales embedded in urban history or are hard-set on formalist documentaries like Architecton (MIFF 2024), Wilson delivers it all in slabs. As entertainment grows gimmickier and ever more disposable, The History of Concrete offers a rich, hearteningly humane meditation on life and death, on the things we either leave in ruins or care enough to fix.
films.awards: Fans of HBO comedy series How To With John Wilson will rejoice that the director’s Sundance-premiering debut feature continues along his curious perambulation.
films.recommendation: Letterboxd 3.91/5；纪录片、喜剧 / 纪录片单元 / 维州首映。推荐理由：家庭关系、亲密关系、记忆与历史、失去与哀悼、音乐与表演、纪录片视角。奖项/展映线索：圣丹斯、首映

films.title: Better Go Mad in the Wild
films.synopsis: Karlovy Vary’s Crystal Globe winner is an enchanting portrait of solitude and free-spirited abandon following eccentric twin brothers living in the Czech wilderness.
films.description: In a farmstead deep in the Šumavan Mountains, situated near Czechia’s western borders with Austria and Germany, live identical twins František and Ondřej Klišík. Now in their sixties, the brothers exist in bucolic bliss: drinking, bickering, swimming in glacial lakes and wandering – sometimes in the nude – through nearby forests. On other occasions, they commune with the animals in their midst: their chickens and cows; their dog Joint; and their majestic bull Nandy, whose voiceover recounts the brothers’ storied pasts and current concerns. Informed by the eponymous book by journalist Aleš Palán and showered with three Czech Lion Awards as well as the top prize at the Karlovy Vary International Film Festival, Better Go Mad in the Wild is a dreamy and poetic glimpse into a life of wilful seclusion. Produced over a five-year period, during which director Miro Remo and his crew spent 60 days with the twins, it leverages its hybrid form – not quite essay film or observational documentary – to transport viewers to a world both elemental and unmoored from modern society. It’s a stunning work propelled by emotion and tone, joining František and Ondřej as they muse on mortality, their misadventures and the dreams keeping their maverick ways alive.
films.awards: Karlovy Vary’s Crystal Globe winner is an enchanting portrait of solitude and free-spirited abandon following eccentric twin brothers living in the Czech wilderness. | Informed by the eponymous book by journalist Aleš Palán and showered with three Czech Lion Awards as well as the top prize at the Karlovy Vary International Film Festival, Better Go Mad in the Wild is a dreamy and poetic...

films.title: Palimpsest: The Story of a Name
films.synopsis: Through a canny postcolonial lens, a filmmaker whose Western name is at odds with her Chinese heritage peels back conflicting layers of family history.
films.description: “All my life I’ve been asked about my family name,” reflects Mary Stephen ( Justocoeur , MIFF 1981). She was born in British Hong Kong to Hilda and Henry Stephen, respectively known in earlier life as Chan Tik-Fong and Hilda Yik. Like their filmmaker daughter, both parents had a creative streak: she wrote poetry, while he was an avid amateur filmmaker and journal keeper, documenting their family life extensively. Poring over her father’s records, Stephen, as the film’s narrator, uncovers all manner of likely tall tales – including a claim of an Indigenous Australian grandfather – and meditates upon the mechanisms and functions of such mythologisation. Together with her parents, Stephen emigrated to Montreal at age 15 before moving to Paris, where she would establish herself as the editor of some of Éric Rohmer’s most cherished films, including A Tale of Winter and A Summer’s Tale . Her own directorial work, largely unseen for decades, has recently enjoyed a much-deserved revival thanks to the restoration of her dreamy 1978 debut, Shades of Silk (MIFF 2026). Stephen’s latest film, which received two Golden Horse Awards, proves a richly personal new entry in an oeuvre dedicated to the exploration of identity across borders.
films.awards: Stephen’s latest film, which received two Golden Horse Awards, proves a richly personal new entry in an oeuvre dedicated to the exploration of identity across borders.
films.recommendation: Letterboxd 3.60/5；纪录片 / 纪录片单元 / 澳洲首映。推荐理由：家庭关系、政治与社会现实、记忆与历史。奖项/展映线索：获奖

films.title: Medea
films.synopsis: In her only film role, opera singer Maria Callas stars as the infamous sorceress in Pier Paolo Pasolini’s mesmeric take on the immortal Greek tragedy.
films.description: High priestess Medea, the granddaughter of the Greek sun god Helios, has her life fatefully ruptured when Jason and the Argonauts arrive on her homeland of Colchis to steal the Golden Fleece. Together, she and Jason abscond to Corinth, where she attempts to relinquish her spiritual past and subsume herself in the role of dutiful wife and mother to their two sons. When Jason abandons her to follow a more politically beneficial marriage, Medea is driven to a shocking act of vengeance. In this visually stunning adaptation of Euripides’s canonical work, Pier Paolo Pasolini infuses the mythical tragedy with biting colonial allegory. A close friend of the visionary director, the legendary Maria Callas – dressed in brilliant costumes by Piero Tosi, who had previously worked with her on opera productions – delivers a powerhouse performance of diva proportions in the title role. The dramatic landscapes of Türkiye’s Central Anatolian region are further emphasised by the production design of Dante Ferretti, who worked on eight of Pasolini’s films before his Oscar-winning Hollywood career.
films.awards: High priestess Medea, the granddaughter of the Greek sun god Helios, has her life fatefully ruptured when Jason and the Argonauts arrive on her homeland of Colchis to steal the Golden Fleece.

films.title: Middle of the Night
films.synopsis: In this forward-thinking drama, a widowed businessman becomes involved with a much younger employee at his textiles factory.
films.description: Jerry Kingsley (played by two-time Academy Award winner Fredric March), a recently bereaved Manhattan textiles manufacturer, is encouraged by all around him to take a new wife. What his family and buddies don’t expect is that the 56-year-old man’s affections will land on Betty Preisser (Kim Novak), the 24-year-old divorced receptionist who works at his factory. Even as the two tentatively pursue this May–December romance, they soon grasp the complexities of sharing the relationship with their families and friends. Marilyn Monroe had originally campaigned to play Betty after seeing the Broadway production on which the film was based, but the role instead went to Novak, keen to prove her acting chops and break free of the bombshell image Columbia Pictures had created for her. Adapted for the screen by playwright Paddy Chayefsky ( Marty ) and shot in striking black-and-white, the emotional performances from March and Novak – who cites it as her favourite turn – devastate. Premiering in Competition at the 1959 Cannes Film Festival, where Novak’s appearance on the Croisette was among the event’s most documented, Middle of the Night is a deeply human and compassionate portrait of people reaching for connection in a world determined to judge them for it.
films.awards: Jerry Kingsley (played by two-time Academy Award winner Fredric March), a recently bereaved Manhattan textiles manufacturer, is encouraged by all around him to take a new wife.
films.recommendation: Letterboxd 3.69/5；剧情 / Kim Novak: Hollywood Heretic。推荐理由：家庭关系、亲密关系、政治与社会现实、纪录片视角。奖项/展映线索：获奖

films.title: The Dreamed Adventure
films.synopsis: Almost a decade after Western , Valeska Grisebach returns with this similarly rough-hewn, simmering tale of border town intrigue that claimed the Cannes Jury Prize.
films.description: The ostensibly sleepy town of Svilengrad sits on the Bulgarian border, with Türkiye to the east and Greece to the south. The laconic Said has hardly been there a day when his car is stolen. Not that it appears to bother him unduly – this rangy former local is an unruffled sort, returned, it seems, to broker a shady fuel deal. A chance encounter reunites him with Veska, an old acquaintance who’s in town helming an archaeological dig. Her attraction to Said sees her venture dangerously deep into Svilengrad’s boozy, machismo-laden underworld. Awarded the Jury Prize at Cannes, The Dreamed Adventure is as sinewy, assured and enigmatic as Said, played by the striking non-professional actor Syuleyman Letifov ( Western , MIFF 2017), while the brilliant Yana Radeva gradually takes centre stage in the film as Veska. In this sprawling, arid noir, the latent threat of violence charges even minor gestures, whether it be the pouring of another drink, the lighting of another cigarette or the simple act of leaning back in a plastic lawn chair. It’s a subtly masterful work, well worth the long wait.
films.awards: Almost a decade after Western , Valeska Grisebach returns with this similarly rough-hewn, simmering tale of border town intrigue that claimed the Cannes Jury Prize. | Awarded the Jury Prize at Cannes, The Dreamed Adventure is as sinewy, assured and enigmatic as Said, played by the striking non-professional actor Syule...
films.recommendation: Letterboxd 3.09/5；剧情、惊悚 / 主打佳片 / 维州首映。推荐理由：政治与社会现实、悬疑 / 犯罪张力。奖项/展映线索：评审团奖、获奖、戛纳

films.title: Whispers in May
films.countries: Hong Kong, Netherlands, Sweden, South Korea
films.synopsis: The winner of the main prize at CPH:DOX, this impressionistic hybrid documentary follows a teenage girl on a quest to find a ceremonial skirt.
films.description: Fourteen-year-old Qihuo has just had her first period. In her Nuosu community in south-west China’s remote, mountainous Liangshan district, this milestone signals that she is ready to work in the factories – and, even, for arranged marriage. Tradition also calls for menarche to be commemorated with a “skirt-changing” ceremony, in which garments signifying childhood are replaced. With her parents both working miles away from their village, Qihuo shares her secret with her two best friends; and, together, the girls set out across the mountains to find her a new skirt. Described by director Dongnan Chen as a “reluctant coming-of-age film”, Whispers in May is a poignant documentary – created collaboratively with its protagonists – that could easily be mistaken for a fictional work. As the girls traverse the rugged landscape, trawl through fabrics at local markets and get their palms read by fortune tellers, we witness this precious and fleeting moment of their lives between childhood and adulthood, between freedom and responsibility. In awarding this dreamlike time capsule, the CPH:DOX jury praised it as “a modern-day fairytale that heeds monsters and factories, alike”.
films.awards: The winner of the main prize at CPH:DOX, this impressionistic hybrid documentary follows a teenage girl on a quest to find a ceremonial skirt.
films.recommendation: Letterboxd 3.58/5；纪录片、剧情 / 学校单元 / 澳洲首映。推荐理由：家庭关系、成长与青春、亲密关系、政治与社会现实、纪录片视角。奖项/展映线索：获奖

films.title: The Man With the Golden Arm
films.synopsis: An early-career milestone for Kim Novak, who stars alongside Frank Sinatra, this honest, raw depiction of a man’s addiction to heroin was a shocking outlier in its era of release.
films.description: A landmark of postwar American cinema, 1955 classic The Man With the Golden Arm takes an unflinching look at the psychological impacts of addiction with a startling candour that shattered Hollywood taboos at the time. After a stint getting clean in prison, aspiring jazz drummer Frankie Machine (Frank Sinatra, From Here to Eternity ) returns to Chicago’s chaotic backstreets, where he struggles to rebuild his life within the constraints of a manipulative marriage and the suffocating pull of old habits, battling the allure of heroin. Columbia Pictures agreed to loan Kim Novak to United Artists after Otto Preminger specifically requested to cast her in the role of Molly Novotny, Frankie’s sympathetic girlfriend; it proved to be a breakthrough role for her. Sinatra likewise delivers a career-defining performance, while Eleanor Parker ( The Sound of Music ) is deliciously wicked as Frankie’s seemingly hapless wife. Adapted from Nelson Algren’s uncompromising novel and featuring a groundbreaking jazz score by Elmer Bernstein, Preminger’s Oscar-nominated film – released without the Production Code seal of approval – remains a devastating character study laden with moral ambiguity.
films.awards: A landmark of postwar American cinema, 1955 classic The Man With the Golden Arm takes an unflinching look at the psychological impacts of addiction with a startling candour that shattered Hollywood taboos at the time.
films.recommendation: Letterboxd 3.67/5；剧情 / Kim Novak: Hollywood Heretic。推荐理由：家庭关系、亲密关系、政治与社会现实、音乐与表演。奖项/展映线索：有电影节展映/奖项线索

films.title: Super Nature
films.language: English, French, Thai, Dutch, Icelandic, Punjabi
films.synopsis: In a new twist on nature documentary, the old-school format of super-8 offers a stunningly impressionistic, hopeful vision of our footprint upon the world.
films.description: Forget ultra-high-definition cameras, macro imagery and drone shots. Compiled exclusively from new super-8 footage shot on vintage cameras in 25 countries across five continents, Super Nature offers an unexpectedly rich, gloriously lo-fi view of the world, captured by 40 local volunteer cinematographers – including Australians Oliver Birt, Caitlin Henderson and Fraser Johnston – who filmed whatever caught their eye, providing commentary as they saw fit. From Welsh puffins and Patagonian whales to Italian mountain ranges and Indigenous Brazilian communities, Super Nature is a global love letter to nature, written in all the textures of the human heart. It’s a testament to Ed Sayers’s lifelong passion for super-8 that he could craft such a coherent narrative from these disparate vignettes, shot by super-8 aficionados as well as biologists, conservationists and environmental activists. Sharing his vision was Oscar-winning filmmaker Asif Kapadia ( Amy ; Senna , MIFF 2011), who was so inspired by Sayers’s pitch that he came on board as executive producer. Despite our planet’s obvious challenges, Super Nature is full of compassion and hope for its future, reconnecting us to our world through the nostalgic intimacy and vibrant immediacy of its format.
films.awards: Sharing his vision was Oscar-winning filmmaker Asif Kapadia ( Amy ; Senna , MIFF 2011), who was so inspired by Sayers’s pitch that he came on board as executive producer.
films.recommendation: Letterboxd 3.53/5；纪录片 / 自然世界 / 澳洲首映。推荐理由：家庭关系、成长与青春、亲密关系、政治与社会现实、纪录片视角。奖项/展映线索：获奖

films.title: Extra Geography
films.synopsis: A self-assigned summer project leads to tough lessons in life, love and friendship for a precocious pair of boarding school BFFs.
films.description: Best friends Minna and Flic are on a mission to graduate from their English boarding school and get into Oxbridge. Keenly aware that they’ll need to go the extra mile to increase their chances of selection, the girls set themselves an extracurricular assignment to become more “worldly”. Their goal: to fall in love with the first person they see … and that just happens to be their totally oblivious geography teacher, Miss Delavigne (Alice Englert, Bad Behaviour , MIFF 2023). With a punchy script penned by Miriam Battye and adapted from a short story by Rose Tremain, Extra Geography is a funny, bittersweet ode to teenage-girl friendship in the lineage of films like Ghost World , Clueless and Booksmart . BAFTA-winning TV director Molly Manners’s debut feature is led by remarkable performances from newcomers Galaxie Clear and Marni Duggan as Minna and Flic, whose convincingly cosy friendship is tested by the rocky terrain of adolescence, full of confusing hormones, co-dependency and competitiveness.

films.title: Decorado
films.synopsis: Unicorn Wars director Alberto Vázquez returns with another visionary, Goya Award–winning animated polemic.
films.description: Middle-aged mouse Arnold is unemployed, and his cartoonist wife María’s salary sometimes falls short when the bills arrive. As if these pressures weren’t bad enough, he’s also convinced that the town they live in is a theatre set (or decorado in Spanish). With the all-powerful Almighty Limitless Megacorporative Agency tightening its grip on the townsfolk, Arnold risks losing both his home and his marriage – so he enlists the ghost of his recently deceased best friend Ramiro and the outcast Mr. Mushroom, and sets out to reclaim what’s left of his life. Expanding on Alberto Vázquez’s lauded short of the same name (MIFF 2016), Decorado swaps out the overt violence of his award-winning feature debut Unicorn Wars (MIFF 2022) for a seething sense of menace, capturing the paranoia that can come from being a mere hamster (or mouse) on a wheel. Evoking The Truman Show , Nineteen Eighty-Four and classic Disney, the film is visually bold and narratively breathtaking, tapping into the existential, industrial and psychological crises of a present riddled with inequality and fake news. If all the world’s a stage, this dystopian work dares to ask, are we its actors obliged to keep the show going on?

films.title: Wicker
films.synopsis: Olivia Colman and Alexander Skarsgård lead this cheeky, star-studded and irresistible fable about a cranky fisherwoman and the sexy handwoven husband she wishes for.
films.description: In a seaside village lost to time, a fisherwoman delivers the food relied on by the townsfolk, who barely tolerate her cantankerous presence. Unexpectedly named the next to wed and sick of the disdain, she commissions the local basketmaker to weave her the perfect husband, and he goes above and beyond. When you’re married to the hottest and handiest man around, however, jealousies inevitably flare up. Adapting Ursula Wills-Jones’s 2008 short story, this Sundance-premiering twist on a period love story – directed by Australian-born Eleanor Wilson and filmmaking (as well as real-life) partner Alex Huston Fischer, and including MIFF Accelerator alum David Michôd among its producers – is at once ingenious and charmingly irreverent. But it’s no straw-man depiction of middle-aged sexual wish fulfilment: the film uses its whimsical premise to critique small-mindedness, superstition and overzealous conservatism. Olivia Colman goes all-in for a role that recalls her fiery turn in The Favourite , while Alexander Skarsgård ( Lee , MIFF 2024) is magnetic as a creature with more humanity than his human counterparts – testament to the practical effects by Joe Dunckley ( Avatar ) and WETA Workshop. Capped off with transportive cinematography by Oscar winner Lol Crawley ( The Brutalist ) and counting Peter Dinklage ( The Toxic Avenger , MIFF 2025), Richard E. Grant and Melbourne’s own Elizabeth Debicki ( The Tale , MIFF 2018) among its cast, Wicker is a fantasy romance that asks whether we truly know our heart’s desire.
films.awards: Adapting Ursula Wills-Jones’s 2008 short story, this Sundance-premiering twist on a period love story – directed by Australian-born Eleanor Wilson and filmmaking (as well as real-life) partne...

films.title: Hen
films.synopsis: A chicken crosses the road and discovers adventure on the other side in this offbeat, moving and utterly unique bird’s-eye view on the suffering of the world.
films.description: A poultry transport driver steals a factory-farmed hen, planning to put her in a tasty soup. When he stops his truck at a servo, she escapes, evading a hungry fox and braving a busy highway before getting cooped up in a run-down seaside restaurant. All she wants is to find a safe nest to brood her own chicks – but as she repeatedly strives to find out what becomes of her eggs, she encounters a world in which hens aren’t the only creatures to be exploited. Fans of animal-centred dramas like Andrea Arnold’s Cow (MIFF 2021) and Jerzy Skolimowski’s EO (MIFF 2022) – or even the Oscar-winning animation Flow (MIFF 2024) – will adore this feathery fancy from György Pálfi ( Taxidermia , MIFF 2006). Filmed vividly at chook’s-eye level, its allegory is compelling but never didactic, witty without being gimmicky. There are no animatronics or CGI – just thrilling action and set pieces that need to be seen to be believed, along with the genuine emotion that the inimitable Hungarian auteur draws from an all-star flock of trained birds.
films.awards: ...like Andrea Arnold’s Cow (MIFF 2021) and Jerzy Skolimowski’s EO (MIFF 2022) – or even the Oscar-winning animation Flow (MIFF 2024) – will adore this feathery fancy from György Pálfi ( Taxidermia , MIFF 2006)....

films.title: Nuisance Bear
films.synopsis: As animals and humans collide in the world’s “Polar Bear Capital”, this acclaimed doc pairs gorgeous photography with broader climate change questions.
films.description: Welcome to the snowy Canadian outpost of Churchill, Manitoba, the self-proclaimed “Polar Bear Capital of the World”. Driven towards urban developments by changing climate and the increasing encroachment of ‘civilisation’ into wilderness, the iconic animals arrive as both dangerous predators and photo-op fodder for tourists. As the bears forage for food and scrap for survival, indifferent to the circus that surrounds them, locals debate whether to welcome them, drive them away peacefully or follow a more traditional mode of coexistence informed by Indigenous practices. Adapted from Gabriela Osio Vanden and Jack Weisman’s award-winning 2021 short of the same name, this stirring and beautifully observational film won the U.S. Documentary Grand Jury Prize at Sundance. Blessed with stunning photography of bear and landscape, narrated by Inuit Elder Mike Tunalaaq Gibbons, and highlighted by a playful score from The White Lotus composer Cristóbal Tapia de Veer, this tragicomic portrait of one of the animal realm’s most regal creatures gives rise to bigger conversations about climate change, displacement, migration and the rights of habitation.
films.awards: As animals and humans collide in the world’s “Polar Bear Capital”, this acclaimed doc pairs gorgeous photography with broader climate change questions. | Welcome to the snowy Canadian outpost of Churchill, Manitoba, the self-proclaimed “Polar Bear Capital of the World”.

films.title: Shape of Momo
films.synopsis: In this double Busan award winner, a woman’s urban feminist sensibilities place her at odds with her family’s traditional community.
films.description: Having left her job in Delhi, 32-year-old Bishnu returns to the village in Sikkim where she grew up. She stays in the family home, joining three generations of women: her grandmother, who misses her Dubai-based son; her mother, who juggles domestic and communal pressures; and her pregnant older sister, who is avoiding her husband and in-laws. Bishnu knows she has to help them, but her progressive views clash with those of the townsfolk. Meanwhile, her fondness for local architect Gyan only complicates matters. Winning the Busan International Film Festival’s Songwon Vision and Taipei Film Commission awards, Tribeny Rai’s rousing feature debut tackles the conflict between tradition and modernity by way of one woman’s journey towards forging her own path. Set against breathtaking views of remote north-eastern India, the film recalls the works of Jia Zhang-ke in its dramatisation of how ‘progress’ – seen here through the prevalence of iPads, K-pop and karaoke – can reshape a culture. In her prodigious breakout role, optometrist-turned-actor Gaumaya Gurung injects Bishnu with complexity, playing her as both a maverick with a brattish disregard for custom and a firebrand unshackling herself from patriarchal strictures.

films.title: Time and Water
films.synopsis: Iceland’s glaciers – once imposing, now imperilled – loom over a personal journey through generations in Sara Dosa’s follow-up to Fire of Love .
films.description: The first Icelandic glacier was declared lost to climate change in 2019; recent studies project that, in 200 years, the country will be entirely ice-free. For celebrated author Andri Snær Magnason, the layers of history melting along with the ice have a personal connection: in the 1950s, his grandparents were among the first to explore Iceland’s glaciers, capturing their splendour in incredible colour footage that we see alongside Magnason’s own home movies. Infusing chilly facts with a lyrical sensibility, this awe-inspiring documentary brings big environmental questions into focus through the lens of personal history. It’s a balancing act that director Sara Dosa already perfected in her breakout Fire of Love (MIFF 2022), which shone a new light on the work of pioneering volcanologist couple Katia and Maurice Krafft. Arriving at MIFF via Sundance, SXSW and CPH:DOX, Time and Water is a moving meditation on what once was, and what has already vanished.
films.awards: Arriving at MIFF via Sundance, SXSW and CPH:DOX, Time and Water is a moving meditation on what once was, and what has already vanished.

films.title: We Are Aliens
films.synopsis: This poignant Cannes-premiering animation traces the friendship and falling-out of two boys after an incident at school changes their lives forever.
films.description: Attending the third grade in rural Japan, awkward Tsubasa has trouble connecting with his peers. Luckily, the gregarious Gyotaro invites him to play, and they quickly become firm friends. However, an unexpected event not only drives a wedge between the pair but also destabilises the school’s social hierarchy. These disruptions go on to mark both boys in profound ways, through their adolescent years and well into adulthood. Bowing at Cannes Directors’ Fortnight, Kohei Kadowaki’s stunning and stirringly told directorial debut recalls Hirokazu Kore-eda’s Monster (MIFF 2023) in its preoccupation with the impacts of childhood cruelty and Akira Kurosawa’s Rashomon in its bisected structure representing each protagonist’s point of view, filling in gaps of memory and offering alternative perspectives on proceedings. Rendered using a dynamic, at times dreamlike blend of rotoscoped and hand-drawn 2D animation, We Are Aliens is a beautiful, bittersweet meditation on lost innocence and the internecine fractures that can complicate the experience of growing up.

films.title: Everytime
films.synopsis: Awarded the Un Certain Regard Prize at Cannes, this indelible study of loss joins a fragmented family as they take a trip to heal and confront the past.
films.description: Ella and her daughters Jessie and Melli have booked a trip to Tenerife to take a break from their busy life in Berlin. The night before they’re due to depart, Jessie and her boyfriend Lux head out to a party, where a cocktail of alcohol and drugs leads to tragedy. One year on, the thwarted trip to the Canary Islands is reattempted, but the heartache continues to reverberate beneath seemingly healed exteriors. The third feature from Sandra Wollner eschews the oversimplification that often accompanies the trope of unexpected death, elegantly playing with time and perspective to shed new light on events. The film’s pensive, precise images – shot by Aftersun (MIFF 2022) DOP Gregory Oke – likewise command attention as they distil grief, guilt and their after-effects. Birgit Minichmayr ( Everyone Else , MIFF 2016) is arresting as a bereaved mother whose performance of survival hinders her recovery, while newcomer Lotte Shirin Keiling impresses in embodying Melli’s wide-eyed, almost magical approach to coping with unthinkable loss.
films.awards: Awarded the Un Certain Regard Prize at Cannes, this indelible study of loss joins a fragmented family as they take a trip to heal and confront the past. | Ella and her daughters Jessie and Melli have booked a trip to Tenerife to take a break from their busy life in Berlin.

films.title: Clarissa
films.synopsis: Sophie Okonedo is sublime as a contemporary Nigerian Mrs
films.description: Sophie Okonedo is sublime as a contemporary Nigerian Mrs. Dalloway in a star-stacked drama that deftly revitalises Virginia Woolf’s themes of memory and regret. As elegant Lagos society wife Clarissa (Sophie Okonedo, Mouse , MIFF 2026) prepares to host an opulent party, she remembers one golden summer with the friends of her youth: aspiring writer Peter (Toheeb Jimoh, Ted Lasso ), outspoken Sally (Ayo Edebiri, Theater Camp , MIFF 2023; The Bear ) and cheerful Ugo. Why didn’t she marry the passionate Peter, or explore her attraction to Sally? When Clarissa unexpectedly runs into the now-dispirited expat Peter (David Oyelowo, Selma ), she invites him to the party, where the quartet reunite and reckon with their past choices – choices that were, in their own way, also privileges.

films.title: Imposters
films.synopsis: When a lost little boy is found, his father is sceptical that it’s really him. But don’t be fooled – this mind-bender isn’t really about changelings or revenants.
films.description: New York cop Paul hasn’t been the same since a near-fatal gunshot injury. Together with their baby, Theo, he and his wife Marie move to a quiet suburb in the hope of reviving their failing marriage. But after Theo vanishes mysteriously at his own first birthday party, an odd neighbour – who seems somehow connected to their house – tells the distraught parents about a cave that can “give back things you have lost”. Venturing into the darkness, Marie re-emerges with Theo, having lost all memory of what happened inside the cave. Paul begins to suspect that whatever she’s brought back, it isn’t their son … Writer/director Caleb J. Phillips won SXSW’s Midnight Shorts Jury Award in 2019 for Other Side of the Box . This, his feature debut, also premiered at SXSW, offering a surprisingly speculative twist on a horror trope you thought you knew. Jessica Rothe ( Happy Death Day ) stuns in a wrenching, primal performance as the increasingly erratic Marie, while Charlie Barnett ( Russian Doll ) holds compassion for the otherwise off-puttingly selfish, passive Paul. Shrewd yet slippery, Imposters suggests a true self is whatever we can live with.
films.awards: Phillips won SXSW’s Midnight Shorts Jury Award in 2019 for Other Side of the Box .

films.title: On the Road
films.synopsis: At once intense and intimate, Venice 2025’s Queer Lion winner follows a drifter who goes on the run – and finds a meaningful spark – with a middle-aged truck driver.
films.description: Foolhardy young Veneno makes ends meet by sleeping with drivers at truck stops in exchange for cash or drugs. He seemingly has no family nor long-term plans; so, when one of his clients stiffs him, he decides to hitch a ride to the other side of the country. Enter Muñeco, a hardened fortysomething trucker estranged from his wife and kids, who offers Veneno a seat in his 18-wheeler. As the men drive across northern Mexico, walls come down and feelings develop – but can the pair outrun the demons from Veneno’s past when they give chase? Winning the Queer Lion at Venice as well as the festival’s Orizzonti Award for Best Film, the fifth feature from David Pablos ( The Chosen Ones , MIFF 2015) – produced by Diego Luna ( Y tu mamá también , MIFF 2002) – both fulfils and defies genre expectations with its noir-infused, violent and erotically charged tale of lust, longing and the winding road in search of connection. Newcomers Víctor Miguel Prieto and Osvaldo Sánchez have an undeniable chemistry, embodying Veneno and Muñeco’s shared hunger for the belonging that their hypermasculine, heteronormative blue-collar world has denied them.

films.title: Josephine
films.synopsis: A heartbreaking portrait of innocence lost, this acclaimed Sundance award winner and surefire Oscar contender is one of the most essential films of 2026
films.description: A heartbreaking portrait of innocence lost, this acclaimed Sundance award winner and surefire Oscar contender is one of the most essential films of 2026. Eight-year-old Josephine (Mason Reeves) is out running with her father in San Francisco’s Golden Gate Park when she witnesses a brutal sexual assault. Her parents, Damien (Channing Tatum) and Claire (Gemma Chan), are unsure of how to help their daughter process this experience, working through their own feelings of futility, helplessness and horror. As a court case prosecuting the attacker draws closer, Josephine is haunted not only by the memory of what she saw but also by the presence of the perpetrator, who lingers in her mind – and, she imagines, in her bedroom – like a ghost.
films.awards: A heartbreaking portrait of innocence lost, this acclaimed Sundance award winner and surefire Oscar contender is one of the most essential films of 2026 | A heartbreaking portrait of innocence lost, this acclaimed Sundance award winner and surefire Oscar contender is one of the most essential films of 2026.

films.title: Chronovisor
films.synopsis: In this wild, one-of-a-kind shrine to the wonders of research, an academic falls down a multimedia rabbit hole of hoax, conspiracy and Vatican intervention.
films.description: Béatrice Courte (played by real-life professor Anne Laure Sellier) is a polylingual academic with research interests in the darkest corners of philosophy. When she encounters a short paragraph on the Chronovisor – a device built in the 1950s that claimed to “photograph the past” as a kind of spectral, visual time machine – she soon becomes obsessed. Camping out at libraries, poring over pages and chasing up leads in four different languages, Béatrice is hot on the trail of the machine’s inventor: an eccentric real-life Benedictine monk named Father Pellegrino Ernetti. Is he still alive? Is his machine a hoax? And how is the Catholic Church involved? With its closing crawl listing a bunch of “creditors” including Jorge Luis Borges, Umberto Eco, Roberto Bolaño and Paul Auster, this hyper-literary work of sleuthing offers an experimental, lo-fi riff on reading-room conspiracy classics like The Ninth Gate (MIFF 2000), Neil LaBute’s Possession and even The Da Vinci Code . Neither strictly documentary nor fiction, the debut feature of directors and academics Kevin Walker and Jack Auen fashions an uncanny bricolage from largely authentic books, magazines, journals and degraded videos. Shot on 16mm in a host of shadowy, Gothic libraries, this is an atmospheric, slyly funny oddity that cast a spell on audiences at Rotterdam and New Directors/New Films.
films.awards: Shot on 16mm in a host of shadowy, Gothic libraries, this is an atmospheric, slyly funny oddity that cast a spell on audiences at Rotterdam and New Directors/New Films.
