films.title: School for Defectors
films.synopsis: Spending a year in a school for children of North Korean refugees, Jeremy Workman’s documentary is as humanistic as it is intimate.
films.description: In many respects, the pupils at Jangdaehyun School are like any other teens: they stress about exams and getting into college; they dance on TikTok; they joke and laugh with friends. And they dream about their futures – a radical act when you’re born in North Korea. But Jangdaehyun is in South Korea, and its 20 boarders are all children of defectors from across the 38th parallel. Surrounded by the overwhelming promise of cosmopolitan democracy and buffeted by the often-negative attitudes of their new neighbours, the school provides a haven where these kids are free to discover who they are and who they want to be. Over the course of a year, Jeremy Workman ( Secret Mall Apartment , MIFF 2024) embedded himself within the school with permission from the students, their parents and teachers, and filmed. The Jangdaehyun community became his collaborators, welcoming him and his crew into their day-to-day lives as well as sharing their harrowing memories, hopes and ambitions. In small moments and in defiant acts, School for Defectors gives empathetic voice to an unseen generation who refuse to let their pasts define them.

films.title: Sour Minnows
films.synopsis: This surrealist stoner noir follows two LA roommates whose reality starts slipping after they witness a shocking scene of pavement worship one sunny afternoon.
films.description: Walking home from grocery shopping one afternoon, roommates Ricky and Tepper stumble across a disturbing scene: six grown men lying on the ground, feverishly licking the pavement. From that moment on, everything changes for Ricky, Tepper and even Aura, the girl Ricky is newly dating. Time slips, space bends, reality morphs and little slimy minnows start to appear in Ricky’s pockets. The deeper Ricky investigates, the more he starts to believe that the three of them may be under the control of a mysterious entity known as “The Yellow Thing”. A stoner hangout movie with Lynchian stylings and an experimental spirit, Sour Minnows comes straight to MIFF following its world premiere at Montreal’s Fantasia International Film Festival, where it screened in competition. Directed, written, edited and shot by Harrison Atkins – who also edited Emily the Criminal (MIFF 2022) and Madeline’s Madeline (MIFF 2018) this film is his second feature after 2015’s Lace Crater , on which MIFF 2026 Bright Horizons juror Joe Swanberg served as a producer. Equal parts goofily funny and chillingly existential, this quintessentially LA trip is capably led by David Brown ( Jury Duty ), Chase Williamson ( John Dies at the End , MIFF 2013) and Suzanna Son ( Red Rocket ).

films.title: Sukundimi Walks Before Me
films.countries: Australia, New Zealand, Papua New Guinea
films.synopsis: Papuan Indigenous communities mobilise to fight a planned mine on the sacred Sepik River in this stirring portrait of ecoactivism.
films.description: When an Australian mining conglomerate announces plans to build a huge copper/gold mine and toxic-waste dam on Papua New Guinea’s Sepik River, local communities fear pollution and ecological degradation. The “Amazon of the Asia Pacific” snakes for over 1100 kilometres through virgin rainforest, and is one of the few remaining large rivers in the world whose water remains drinkable. It’s also home to tens of thousands, who’ve lived along the river for millennia, drawing on it for sustenance and spiritual lore. Led by the charismatic Emmanuel Peni, these communities begin to mobilise, coming together to fight the forces of old colonial power structures, extractive economic practices, multinational lobbying and the supposed inevitability of ‘progress’. At once an intimate study of community and a rousing work of cine-activism, this boots-on-the-ground – or canoes-on-the-water – documentary offers a regional David vs Goliath story with deep ecological repercussions. Melanesian New Zealander filmmaker Matasila Freshwater (one of the directors of Vai , MIFF 2019) and Australian documentarian Lachlan McLeod ( Clean , MIFF Closing Night 2022) collaborate with community members, their cameras invited to witness conversations, meetings and planning sessions. Filled with rich cinematography, Papuan folk songs and historical newsreels, Sukundimi Walks Before Me is a vivid portrait of virtuous defiance.

films.title: Summer Tour
films.synopsis: In this tribute to one of music’s most enduring, community-minded fan bases, head out on the road with the true believers who follow a Grateful Dead revival act around the US.
films.description: Jerry and Annie are young and in love – with each other, and with the music of the Grateful Dead. Setting out on the road to catch every show on successor band Dead & Company’s farewell tour in 2023, they journey across America and deep into the heart of Deadhead fandom. Meeting fans young and old and participating in the marketplaces and gatherings that sprout around the shows, they discover a true community, alternative ways of living and what the music means to a wideranging cross-section of Deadheads. Mischa Richter’s “love letter” to the communal roadshow of Grateful Dead tours is a deeply personal doc from a committed Dead devotee – as are two of the film’s producers, Chloë Sevigny ( Magic Farm , MIFF 2025) and Emily Mortimer ( The Party , MIFF 2017). Filmed on 16mm and from deep within the community of fans, Summer Tour isn’t about the band – who are never seen performing – but those who follow them. It adds up to a warm-hearted hymn to hippie culture that doubles as an exploration of how people find meaning, and belonging, in art.

films.title: Sweet Milk Lake
films.synopsis: After being mistaken for his brother, a young trans man adapts to a precarious new life in his estranged father’s rural town.
films.description: City boy Jake is at a loose end. Unemployed, single and holed up in a granny flat behind his family home, he lives in the shadow of his cisgender twin brother, successful real estate agent Sam. On an impulse, he takes a trip to the remote country town of Sweet Milk, where his terminally ill, estranged father (Kieran Darcy-Smith) still lives, totally unaware that Jake has transitioned. When his dad mistakes him for Sam and Jake misses the moment to correct him, an unforeseen opportunity lands in his lap: to fit in as masculine in a place where nobody suspects he’s trans. Adopting Sam’s identity, he manages to connect with his father for the first time, while a budding romance with local Toby (Hunter Page-Lochard, Kid Snow , MIFF 2024) – himself an outsider in this macho setting – offers further reinvigoration. But the longer Jake spends in Sweet Milk, the more tangled the web he weaves. How long can he hold on to his secret, and what will happen if the truth gets out? Drawing on his own experiences, debut filmmaker Harvey Zielinski also stars in the dual roles of Jake and Sam in this fish-out-of-water dramedy supported by the MIFF Premiere Fund – a raw, sensitive tale about the long road to self-acceptance and the lies we feel we need to tell to fit in.
films.awards: ...in the dual roles of Jake and Sam in this fish-out-of-water dramedy supported by the MIFF Premiere Fund – a raw, sensitive tale about the long road to self-acceptance and the lies we feel we need to tell to fit in....

films.title: The Fence
films.synopsis: With this thriller headlined by Isaach de Bankolé and Matt Dillon, Claire Denis returns to Africa – and to the profound, prickly themes that have defined her oeuvre.
films.description: Overseeing a major West African construction site, Horn (Matt Dillon) has a lot on his plate: the imminent transition to new Chinese financiers; the arrival of his much younger wife (Mia McKenna Bruce, How to Have Sex , MIFF 2023); a tetchy engineer (Tom Blyth, Benediction ); and, most pressingly, the on-the-job death of a local worker. As night falls, the deceased’s brother Alboury (Isaach de Bankolé, The Brutalist ) arrives like an apparition. He demands to take possession of his sibling’s body, and won’t accept “no” – or bribes, threats or delays – for an answer. One of the true living legends of art cinema, Claire Denis ( Stars at Noon , MIFF 2022) has made a host of films sensuously and dramatically interrogating the European footprint in postcolonial Africa, including her magnum opus, Beau Travail (MIFF 2000), and two previous collaborations with de Bankolé in 1988’s Chocolat (MIFF 2000) and 2009’s White Material . For this late-career work, she has adapted a 1979 play by Bernard-Marie Koltès, whose dialogue-rich scenes mount a surreal, uneasy examination of exploitation, power and posturing.

films.title: The Latcher
films.synopsis: Accelerator Lab alum James Hunter makes his feature debut with this psychological thriller about motherhood and the monstrous clutches of grief.
films.description: American midwife Alice lives in an isolated house in rural Australia. Worn out by her job and wearied by an unspoken tragedy, she experiences waking nightmares and starts sensing an eerie presence creeping in from the shadowy bushland. As desolation overwhelms her, and with the threat inching closer, her only refuge is the very home where she lost everything. Can she vanquish her demons before they corrode what’s left of her sense of self? Inspired by horror classics Rosemary’s Baby and Don’t Look Now as well as fairytales and folklore, this startling, thematically complex feature debut uses atmosphere and symbolism to explore the repercussions of loss, silence and solitude. Informed by interviews with bereavement midwives and a mother who had lost her baby, director James Hunter ( Nest , MIFF 2021) prioritised visual and narrative restraint to create “an uncanny feeling of unease”; this laid the foundations for Lucy Lovegrove’s affecting lead performance, which brims with polished physicality and nuanced interiority. Gripping and full of gravitas, The Latcher portrays one woman’s unravelling and her eventual journey towards healing.

films.title: The Oligarch and the Art Dealer
films.synopsis: The true story of one of the world’s richest men and one of the 21st-century art world’s greatest frauds. Or is it?
films.description: Unfolding like a Hollywood thriller, The Oligarch and the Art Dealer unpacks with exquisite precision all the shady details of the Bouvier Affair: a high-profile court case between Russian businessman Dmitry Rybolovlev, the notoriously private owner of the world’s most expensive art collection, and his former trusted art dealer and friend Yves Bouvier, a maestro of freeports – duty-free tax havens-cum-storage lockers for the ultra-wealthy’s Picassos, da Vincis, Klimts and Magrittes. It’s a case that shocked the world, not least because it forced Rybolovlev – a man without a phone number, email address or computer – into the open and shone a spotlight on the clandestine dealings and deliberate secrets of the global elite. In this engrossing documentary, Andreas Dalsgaard digs into the decade-long battle that began when Rybolovlev accused Bouvier of a billion-dollar scam, blowing up the once-symbiotic relationship that had helped both men to stratospheric financial echelons. Approaching the scandal with an exacting eye, The Oligarch and the Art Dealer allows each man to make his case – Bouvier directly; the elusive Rybolovlev through his lawyers – and the audience to draw their own conclusions. But when the stakes are this high, and the eye-watering sums behind them are even higher, can you trust anyone’s story?

films.title: The Siege of Paradise
films.synopsis: Scoring a Special Jury Mention at Tribeca, this documentary turns its lens on overtourism in Italy’s Cinque Terre, dubbed “the most beautiful place in the world”.
films.description: On the Italian Riviera lies Cinque Terre, whose centuries-old villages and postcard-perfect beaches attract around four million holiday-makers – including countless social media influencers – annually. It’s become one of the most photographed places in the world, but this fame also concerns the 4000 individuals who call it home. Some, like winemaker Bartalo and restaurateur Carmelo, have resigned themselves to this yearly influx that finances their region. Others, like fisherman Guido, lament its toll on tradition, while mediators like politician Fabrizia must weigh profitability against sustainability. Sanatorium (MIFF 2025) director Gar O’Rourke returns with another elegant exploration of culture and place, this time exploring tourism as a double-edged sword bringing both wealth and threats to residents’ way of life. Rather than cold statistics, the film grounds its investigation on compelling firsthand accounts from locals, along with two lifestyle influencers whose insights reveal Cinque Terre’s simultaneous value and expendability for visitors. Meanwhile, DOP Lukas Gut – recipient of Tribeca’s Special Jury Mention for Cinematography in a Documentary Feature – captures Cinque Terre’s painterly radiance, completing this robust, handsomely realised account of #travel’s true cost.
films.awards: Scoring a Special Jury Mention at Tribeca, this documentary turns its lens on overtourism in Italy’s Cinque Terre, dubbed “the most beautiful place in the world”. | Meanwhile, DOP Lukas Gut – recipient of Tribeca’s Special Jury Mention for Cinematography in a Documentary Feature – captures Cinque Terre’s painterly radiance, completing this...

films.title: The Way We Move
films.synopsis: A trailblazing ASL interpreter trains fast-fingered protégés to embody live acoustics in this paean to the Deaf community’s bond with music.
films.description: Amber Galloway specialises in live American Sign Language interpretation for music festivals and concerts. You might recognise her hip-hop swagger from viral online videos: she captures complex instrumentation and wordplay with her whole body, using facial expressions to evoke tones and moods. Now, this accessibility trailblazer is training new ASL music interpreters, including striving young Puerto Rican father Julian Ortiz and Angela “AV” Vilavong, a queer Laotian from Oakland. Can they master this complex skill in time to join Galloway onstage at Austin City Limits Music Festival? As her students wrestle with live interpreting’s complex cognitive demands and poetic creative choices, Galloway’s pedagogical flair is treated with due admiration by French directorial duo Vanessa Dumont and Nicolas Davenel. For their part, Ortiz’s and Vilavong’s gifts are also evident (the former went on to live-interpret Bad Bunny’s Super Bowl half-time show, while the latter has been onstage with Céline Dion and The Chemical Brothers). But this sensitive documentary deals with tough emotions, too: Galloway is grieving her son Elijah, who was himself a promising interpreter, as she tries to pull his surviving twin Joshua from a depressive spiral. Equally, we see how Galloway and her protégés’ proudly queer, ancestral and Deaf identities help them express music’s life-affirming power. Turns out the body doesn’t just keep the score; it keeps the beat.
films.recommendation: 暂无 Letterboxd 评分；纪录片 / 音乐电影 / 澳洲首映。推荐理由：家庭关系、音乐与表演、纪录片视角。奖项/展映线索：暂无明确获奖信息，主要按评分、题材和导演风格推荐

films.title: Tina Arena: Unravel Me
films.synopsis: How do you separate a Melbourne-born Oz music treasure and cosmopolitan star from a resilient woman? Follow the threads of her story, in her own words.
films.description: Tina Arena has been in the music business for 50 years; after 25 albums, she’s had enough eras to make other pop stars envious. Born in suburban Melbourne to Sicilian parents, Filippina Arena became Australia’s darling in 1976, aged eight, on Channel Ten’s prime-time variety show Young Talent Time . Her breakout 1990 pop single ‘I Need Your Body’ raunchily repudiated “Tiny Tina”, and she went on to score a soaring run of 90s hits … but self-doubt, misogyny and tall-poppy syndrome drove her to reinvent herself repeatedly in the UK and France: taking to the stage; seeking adventurous collaborations; and singing in Italian, French and Spanish. Behind Tina the power balladeer and queer icon is the woman her family still calls Pina. This world-premiering documentary from Adrian Russell Wills ( Kindred , MIFF 2023) unravels her vulnerable yet tenacious private self in candid, dryly funny conversation. Also appearing in new and archival interviews are Tina’s two sisters; her mentor Johnny Young; and collaborators and admirers including Céline Dion, Marc Anthony, Ronan Keating, Jessica Mauboy, and Jessica and Lisa Origliasso of The Veronicas. In bringing herself undone, Tina movingly shows how music has made and mended her.
films.awards: This world-premiering documentary from Adrian Russell Wills ( Kindred , MIFF 2023) unravels her vulnerable yet tenacious private self in candid, dryly funny conversation.
films.recommendation: 暂无 Letterboxd 评分；纪录片、音乐 / 音乐电影 / 世界首映。推荐理由：家庭关系、音乐与表演、纪录片视角。奖项/展映线索：首映

films.title: Tomorrow I’ll Be Brave
films.synopsis: The anxieties and heart-fluttering feelings of first love are given tender, respectful treatment in this charming coming-of-age tale.
films.description: Karl is 12, short, insecure … and totally in love. Does the much taller, literally out-of-reach Lea like him back? If so, how can he tell? And how is he going to deal with all the feelings that come with a crush in the meantime? An opportunity to understand these nascent feelings arrives when a teacher encourages Karl and his friends to make a nonfiction short film interviewing people about their first loves. While working on this project, Karl channels his longing into his own creative pursuits – taking photographs that he develops in his homemade darkroom, and composing a love song with his cooler older brother – in the hope of impressing Lea. A sweet take on a familiar summertime-coming-of-age narrative, this special film dares to regard the normal, everyday emotions of adolescents as meaningful and worthy of exploration, with director Bernd Sahling placing the camera at eye level with his pint-sized protagonist. Free from nostalgia and never melodramatic or preachy, Tomorrow I’ll Be Brave is perfectly attuned to tweenage feelings.

films.title: Vaka Moana: Ancestors of the Future
films.synopsis: Learn about time-honoured Polynesian conservation practices while exploring the most southerly and remote atoll of the Cook Islands.
films.description: Made in collaboration with Indigenous NGO Te Puna Marama Voyaging Foundation on location in the Cook Islands, Vaka Moana: Ancestors of the Future is a 48-minute immersive 360-degree experience that takes viewers on a journey to the remote atoll of Mangaia. We sail on a recreated version of the Vaka Moana (or ocean-going canoes) that a millennia ago brought the first people to the Polynesian Pacific – the very last stage of the great human migrations across planet Earth. On this journey, we sail with Pwo master Peia Patai, one of only 10 masters of traditional non-instrument navigation (guided by stars, swells, ecological signs). While visiting ancient celestial maps in limestone caves and journeying across the oldest island in Polynesia, we also learn about the Polynesian resource-management strategy of Ra‘ui: to only take what is needed in order to conserve our ecology for the future. Sailing a small boat across the precarious and finite planet Earth, we learn about this ancestral Indigenous sustainable practice that is urgently needed globally. This is an immersive experience with a deep message from Polynesian ancestors and knowledge-holders for the future of us all.
films.recommendation: 暂无 Letterboxd 评分；纪录片 / 世界首映。推荐理由：导演表达与人物处境。奖项/展映线索：暂无明确获奖信息，主要按评分、题材和导演风格推荐

films.title: We Are The Shaggs
films.synopsis: They were ordinary teen girls forced to form a band by their dad – and, thanks to one unlistenable (to some!) album, became icons of outsider art.
films.description: Austin Wiggin’s mother had prophesied his daughters would become a famous band – so, in 1965, he decided to make it happen. He pulled Dot, Helen and Betty out of school; handed them instruments they couldn’t play; suggested they write songs without the first clue how; and named them The Shaggs. For years, they (occasionally accompanied by younger sister Rachel) endured weekly heckling at local gigs in Fremont, New Hampshire, before recording an album, 1969’s Philosophy of the World . When Austin died in 1975, the sisters could finally live regular lives … until the 90s, when they learned The Shaggs had become a cult band whose tastemaker fans included Frank Zappa, Patti Smith and Kurt Cobain. Their grandmother’s prediction had come true, in the most ironic way possible. One Shaggs evangelist is veteran comedy director Ken Kwapis ( Vibes , MIFF 2019; Malcolm in the Middle ). His film explores the strangeness of the sisters’ wildly out-of-tune guitars, eccentric rhythms and unorthodox chords, tracing their path from “the worst band ever” to outsider music stars, while music experts champion their intuitive brilliance. Meanwhile, gentle, bemused surviving Shaggs Dot and Betty muse on the phenomenon they’ve been caught up in.

films.title: Wilderness
films.synopsis: A Melbourne doctor spiralling into full midlife crisis sets out on a primal pilgrimage into the Victorian Alps.
films.description: Allie’s life seems ideal from the outside: she’s a doctor, living with her teenage daughter and successful husband in an opulent modernist home. But she’s sleeping on the couch, hitting the wine hard, smoking again – prone to making bad decisions and picking fights with anyone who crosses her path. In the wake of a serious car accident, Allie runs. Hitting the road and hitting the booze, she sets off with her trusty dog Stanley and is by chance reunited along the way with her estranged friend Kaz, a sound recordist whose work chronicles environmental degradation. Travelling into increasingly remote stretches of alpine wilderness, her journey becomes primal, confronting the pain of the two women’s past and the unspoken realities of their relationship. Set in the picturesque surrounds of Victoria’s High Country, Wilderness is at once a midlife crisis movie about women behaving badly and a parable about listening to the environments that surround us. Built around a bold lead performance by Mirrah Foulkes ( Animal Kingdom ) and strong support from Yael Stone ( Blaze , MIFF 2022), Martin McKenna’s first film in over a decade is a thoughtful exploration of the balance between the personal and the ecological, and how urban landscapes can disconnect us from the planet and from ourselves.

films.title: Yumburra
films.synopsis: Following the Dark Emu phenomenon, Bruce Pascoe pursues sustainable farming and connection to Country in this gentle documentary portrait.
films.description: In the wake of the success – and media furore – that greeted his landmark book Dark Emu , writer Bruce Pascoe retreated to his tract of land, a piece of Yuin Country in East Gippsland, near the Mallacoota Inlet and right by the New South Wales border. Seeking to put his teachings on First Peoples agriculture into practice, Pascoe and his partner Lyn Harwood set about building the infrastructure for sustainable farming, healing their strained relationship and deepening their connection to Country. Yumburra is shot in observational style by Grace McKenzie, who’s previously charted farming, landscape and community in the documentaries Audrey of the Alps and In the Land of Wolves (MIFF 2018). Following on from Allan Clarke’s 2023 documentary The Dark Emu Story and naturally intersecting with Pascoe and Harwood’s 2024 book Black Duck: A Year at Yumburra , it’s a film furthering explorations of Indigenous wisdom, traditional land management and the cultural conversations we need to have. At once a character study of one of Australia’s great writers – and great mavericks – and an unhurried depiction of life on the land, Yumburra makes a quiet, unassuming case for the lessons we can learn from elders and the environment alike.

films.title: Johanna d’Arc of Mongolia
films.synopsis: In Ulrike Ottinger’s ultimate ethnographic odyssey, a group of women travelling by train are taken hostage by a Mongolian princess.
films.description: The carriages of the Trans-Siberian Railway have never seen such a theatrical ensemble of strangers: the chic, high-society ethnologist Lady Windermere (Delphine Seyrig) can speak several languages; American Broadway star Fanny Ziegfeld (Gillian Scalici) is always threatening to break into song; Russian cabaret trio the Kalinka Sisters are always breaking into song; the reserved German schoolteacher Frau Müller-Vohwinkel (Irm Hermann) has a quiet but curious eye; and Giovanna (Inés Sastre) is a naive young woman with little else but a rucksack, a walkman and a yearning for adventure. After transferring to the Trans-Mongolian Railway, their fantastical train journey makes a sharp turn when a group of mounted Mongolian warriors under the command of Princess Ulun Iga (Xu Re Huar) take them as captives to their nomadic summer village. There, the women observe and attempt to adapt to the customs of their new society. Cast with Western actors and Mongolian non-professionals, Ulrike Ottinger’s 1989 epic was the first feature film permitted to be made in Inner Mongolia by a Western director. An ever-curious artist who would soon after return to film the people, environment and cultural customs of this land for her eight-hour 1992 documentary Taiga , Ottinger has stated that Johanna d’Arc of Mongolia “is not trying to create an exotic image, but to explore the issue of cultural transfer”. Depicting a dazzling clash of traditions and a stunning assemblage of colours and costumes against a vast landscape, this masterwork was frequent collaborator Seyrig’s final performance onscreen.

films.title: Rapid Eye Movement
films.synopsis: How can we create a collective dream? This immersive performance stages a sensory journey through the various states of sleep and consciousness.
films.description: Rapid Eye Movement invites audiences to explore dreamlike landscapes constructed from fragments captured in the physical world. Dreams are the raw material, while the human mind and body serve as the instruments. “Rapid eye movement” refers to the final phase of the five sleep cycles – the dream stage, just before waking – when elements from everyday life resurface and blend into a parallel reality. Ahead of the show, the artists launched an online form to collect dreams from the public. Anyone could contribute to the project, sharing dreams and nightmares, describing visions, or submitting images and sounds. Using these textual and visual elements, the artists have juxtaposed scenes and constructed intricate 3D architectures – abstract, stylised dream worlds that are at once familiar and surreal – combining creations and generative AI and merging music and live images. The resulting immersive performance blurs the boundaries between reality and the dream world in real time, accompanied by a film presenting key moments from the artists’ experimental process.

films.title: 29 Hour Long Birthday
films.synopsis: Mark Jenkin’s thorny postcard from New York and its surrounds presents a world of jackhammers, MAGA caps and celebrity spotting.
films.description: While his breakout feature Enys Men (MIFF 2022) was wowing the international festival circuit, Mark Jenkin crafted this melancholic 2022 dispatch from his holiday in New York, a city whose familiar sights and sounds are rendered in the filmmaker’s signature super-8 style. Inspired by reminiscences of movies and punctuated with a spot of celebrity spotting, the film finds a city in the grip of sociopolitical chaos.

films.title: A Soil A Culture A River A People
films.synopsis: In a dystopian future, a silent, observant Chinese man wanders through a simulacrum of a German city.

films.title: A Very Easy Death
films.synopsis: Named after Simone de Beauvoir’s 1964 memoir, this dreamlike, experimental montage muses on bereavement.
films.description: Fifty years ago, Mary Stephen used her editor’s sensibility to meditate poetically on her mother’s death through still images, 16mm film and song. Hilda emerges in oneiric archival glimpses: her stylish youth; waltzing at home with Mary’s young-adult brother; the achingly empty hospital room where she died. Her daughter’s hand intrudes into the frame in a yearning, impossible caress. Originally, Stephen made only four copies of this short for her siblings, but this generous 2025 restoration shares her memories with everyone.

films.synopsis: Where do we go when we zone out, and how do we return to reality? Zone in – your own way – through a mindful flow of sonic textures and visual motifs.
films.description: When we let our minds drift, where are we wandering? Find out by taking a richly interior journey from ‘no-where’ to ‘now-here’, inspired by Taoism’s enigmatic ‘way’, the projection of illusion from Buddhism and Carl Gustav Jung’s meditative process of active imagination. Fragmented mixed-media stop motion guides your attention from absence to presence, like the palms of hands slowly opening and closing. Everyone takes something different from this intriguing, associative work, and that’s the quiet beauty of thought itself. Explore your own subconscious, and find your way refreshingly back to the present moment.

films.title: The Story of Documentary Film (Part 1)
films.synopsis: The premier chronicler of all things cinematic returns with his latest plunge into film history – this time, shining a projectionist’s lamp on more than a century of reality-based storytelling
films.description: The premier chronicler of all things cinematic returns with his latest plunge into film history – this time, shining a projectionist’s lamp on more than a century of reality-based storytelling. From 1895’s Workers Leaving the Lumière Factory through the cinematic achievements of Leni Riefenstahl, Agnès Varda, Frederick Wiseman and Abbas Kiarostami, the history of film can’t be told without documentary. Interrogating “observational films, campaigning movies, portrait docs, archive-based films, personal essays, music films, journey documentaries, experimental and animated non-fiction cinema”, passionate celluloid historian Mark Cousins ( Women Make Film: A New Road Movie Through Cinema , MIFF 2020) turns his lens on the vast corpus of documentary films. Fashioning unexpected connections and highlighting overlooked filmmakers, he reframes our ideas of truth, power and the pursuit of onscreen beauty.
films.recommendation: 暂无 Letterboxd 评分；纪录片 / 电影之梦 / 维州首映。推荐理由：记忆与历史、音乐与表演、纪录片视角。奖项/展映线索：暂无明确获奖信息，主要按评分、题材和导演风格推荐

films.description: A uniquely tactile exploration of time and light, captured on 16mm film with hundreds of lenses. Using a handcrafted time slice camera equipped with 293 lenses, Belgian artist Victor Van Rossem resurrects a vintage photographic technique to create an incredibly immersive depiction of light on film, striking and almost spectral in its strange optical pull. In the film’s otherworldly space – its 3D tangle of colour and movement – light itself appears to become a physical object.

films.description: Each year at the Melbourne International Film Festival, a panel of guests gather to dissect the highs and lows of the zeitgeist. Host Michael Sun will be joined by two of MIFF’s visiting Critics Campus mentors – Los Angeles–based film programmer and critic Miriam Bale and writer and creator/curator of Black Film Archive Maya S. Cade, who originally hails from New Orleans – along with Melbourne’s own Harvey Zielinski, the writer, director and star of MIFF Bright Horizons Competition film Sweet Milk Lake . They’ll discuss the most overrated, underrated and personal–favourite moments in movies, TV, podcasts and books of the past 12 months. If all goes to plan, there’ll be hot takes, heated discussions and great recommendations to enjoy post-festival. Miriam Bale is a critic and film programmer. She has written for The New York Times , the New York Daily News , W Magazine , Gagosian Quarterly , The New Republic , Film Comment , IndieWire , Sight and Sound and many other publications. She was the artistic director of the Indie Memphis Film Festival in Tennessee, and has curated multiple repertory film programs worldwide.

films.title: Documentary Shorts 1
films.synopsis: Eye-opening nonfiction work from every corner of the world
films.description: Eye-opening nonfiction work from every corner of the world. Experience a hypnotic ride through Australia’s salt-lake speed-racing subculture, watch birds take poetic flight over Spain during lockdown, and go inside the world of Tamil Nadu tuktuk drivers as they battle industry sexism. These short films prove that nothing moves us quite like portraits of real life.

films.title: Footy Shorts
films.synopsis: MIFF and the AFL, supported by VicScreen, present five short documentaries centred on Australia’s game.
films.description: A group of young Chinese Australian footballers come together; the first openly queer ex-AFL player steps back onto the field; a community fights for the safety and dignity of its children; a former AFLW player traces a path from her heroes to the new generation of African Australian stars; and cheer squad members prepare to face a club they haven’t beaten in a decade. Films in this package: Carn the Chinese! (dir. Angela How), For the Boys (dir. Mitchell Withers), Invincible Spirit (dir. Mark Thomson, David Callow), KURA: An African AFL Story (dir. Akec Makur Chuot, Meg Duncan) and North Melbourne Cheer Squad (dir. Jasper Caverly, Andrew Goode).

films.title: Fulldome Showcase One
films.synopsis: MIFF’s program of jaw-dropping fulldome screenings at the Melbourne Planetarium is back.
films.description: Don’t miss this rare opportunity to take in a truly unique cinematic experience, lying back and immersing yourself in the stereo surround sound and super high-resolution images projected onto the 180-degree hemispherical dome ‘screen’ above your head. In the first of two showcases pairing incredible immersive experiences exploring the world around us, embark on a mesmerising odyssey into the world of trees, unveiling the breathtaking biodiversity flourishing within the woods, and explore the most southerly and remote atoll of the Cook Islands, Mangaia.

films.title: Member Screening – Dead Man's Wire
films.synopsis: You are invited to an exclusive First Glance MIFF Member preview of Dead Man's Wire on Tuesday 23 June, thanks to our friends at Madman Entertainment . The morning of February 8, 1977, Anthony G. “Tony” Kiritsis, 44, entered the office of Richard O. Hall, president of the Meridian Mortgage Company, and took him hostage with a sawed-off 12-gauge shotgun wired with a “dead man’s wire” from the trigger to the Hall’s head. This is the true story of the stand-off that took the world by storm as Tony demanded $5 million, no charges or prosecution, and a personal apology from the Halls for cheating him out of what he was “owed.” A film by Gus Van Sant starring Bill Skarsgård, Dacre Montgomery, Colman Domingo and Al Pacino. Bookings: Bookings for this screening will open at 6 pm on Wednesday 27 May – If tickets are booked out, please join the waitlist on the booking page. Seating: There are NO allocated seats for MIFF Member screenings – It's first-come, first-served. Please arrive on time to select your desired seats.

films.title: Modern Moviemaking
films.synopsis: From first feature to 20th, our panel of emerging and established directors will discuss making movies in the current micro-positive moment and beyond.
films.description: Take a temp check on the current indie scene and what it’s like making a film today – whether it’s your debut or you’re a couple of decades deep. Discussing their recent projects and what might lie ahead are celebrated American director Joe Swanberg ( Happy Christmas , MIFF 2014; Drinking Buddies , MIFF 2013), who is screening the Dakota Johnson-starring The Sun Never Sets at MIFF 2026; Hyun Lee ( Asian Girls , MIFF 2018) whose buzzy first feature French Girls is set in Sydney’s fashion world; Maddelin McKenna ( Patterns of the Afternoon , MIFF 2022), with her thrilling first feature Mad Rush , shot on the streets of Melbourne; and phenomenally prolific filmmaker Alice Maio Mackay, whose new feature Our Effed Up World , co-produced by Jane Schoenbrun ( I Saw the TV Glow , MIFF 2024), is her seventh at age 21. Joe Swanberg is an indie mainstay who has directed more than 20 features, including Drinking Buddies , Digging for Fire , Happy Christmas and Nights and Weekends , which he co-directed with Greta Gerwig. In addition to writing and directing all 25 episodes of Netflix’s Chicago anthology Easy , he’s directed in TV for HBO, 20th Century Fox and Netflix.

films.title: Program Launch
films.synopsis: You're invited to join us for the MIFF 2026 Program Launch presented by Mac Forbes . Expect an eye-opening mix of new international features, distinctive Australian stories, Premiere Fund films and boundary-pushing experiences that go far beyond the screen. From immersive XR and unforgettable live events to thought-provoking talks and special presentations, the MIFF 2026 program is packed with discoveries, conversations and moments designed to surprise, inspire and spark excitement. We’re excited to unveil this year’s competition titles for the coveted Bright Horizons Award , supported by VicScreen , showcasing the newest and most compelling voices in cinema. Hosted by MIFF Artistic Director Al Cossar, the evening concludes with the printed program, hot off the press and yours to take home before it lands anywhere else. Event Timings 6.00pm – Doors open, drink on arrival courtesy of Mac Forbes 6.45pm – MIFF 2026 Program Launch presentation 8.00pm – Conclusion PRESENTED BY

films.title: Sovereign Shorts
films.synopsis: MIFF and NITV, supported by VicScreen, present four original and compelling short documentaries exploring Treaty as lived experience
films.description: MIFF and NITV, supported by VicScreen, present four original and compelling short documentaries exploring Treaty as lived experience. In these shorts, all from Victoria-based First Peoples writers and directors, the Wotjobaluk Nations’ underdog fight for recognition is explored; a dialogue with Senator Lidia Thorpe reveals insights into the personal impact of Treaty and sovereignty; dance floors are revealed as spaces of sovereignty, connection and liberation; and we encounter the intertwined lives of three women, each embodying a different stage of First Nations hope in Victoria’s fight for sovereignty.

films.title: The Heart of the Forest
films.synopsis: A mesmerising odyssey into the world of trees, unveiling their interconnected lives and the breathtaking biodiversity flourishing within the woods.
films.description: Deep within the woods stands an ancient and all-knowing oak, the Tree of Life, who serves as both the protagonist and the narrator of the story. The story reveals the forest as a living, breathing organism, full of wisdom, connection and resilience. The Heart of the Forest is a poetic call to protect what remains of our ancient forests and a reminder that their fate is intimately tied to our own.

films.title: The Story of Documentary Film (Part 2)
films.synopsis: The premier chronicler of all things cinematic returns with his latest plunge into film history – this time, shining a projectionist’s lamp on more than a century of reality-based storytelling
films.description: The premier chronicler of all things cinematic returns with his latest plunge into film history – this time, shining a projectionist’s lamp on more than a century of reality-based storytelling. From 1895’s Workers Leaving the Lumière Factory through the cinematic achievements of Leni Riefenstahl, Agnès Varda, Frederick Wiseman and Abbas Kiarostami, the history of film can’t be told without documentary. Interrogating “observational films, campaigning movies, portrait docs, archive-based films, personal essays, music films, journey documentaries, experimental and animated non-fiction cinema”, passionate celluloid historian Mark Cousins ( Women Make Film: A New Road Movie Through Cinema , MIFF 2020) turns his lens on the vast corpus of documentary films. Fashioning unexpected connections and highlighting overlooked filmmakers, he reframes our ideas of truth, power and the pursuit of onscreen beauty.

films.synopsis: Films for freakmaxxers, fetishists and other fans of flickering filth.
films.description: What do you get when you combine films about a lonely woman’s torrid affair with a severed finger; a dying man who visits a sex worker in search of a graphic birth in reverse; and a vicious predator who’s part woman, part insect and all mother? Why MIFF’s WTF Shorts , of course – the festival’s own garden of unholy cinematic delights.

films.title: Écosystème
films.synopsis: An immersive sensory dive into the depths of the sea.
films.description: The underwater world definitely isn’t quiet … and what we hear there tells a story. Through waves, boat engines, marine animals communicating, the icepack cracking or the sound of flowing water – proof of steady melting – this immersive film explores a little-known component of this ecosystem: its soundscape. Écosystème is the result of a long residency combining research and creation. The project originated from a team of marine ecology researchers who wanted to experiment with the links between art and science. Back from a polar mission, they called upon the talents of electronic music producer Maxime Dangles to create new musical material from their underwater sound recordings. To illustrate this sound journey, visual artist Dylan Cote set out to create a dark and enigmatic aquatic universe inhabited by synthetic and minimalist entities. The animated film dives into this world and explores its imaginary lands, guided by a narrative thread showing their progressive disturbance.
